prompt:
Platform ticket. Our secret-rotation cron talks to Vault's KV v2 engine through a pile of hand-rolled curl in bash, and it burned us twice this quarter: once when two rotators raced (last write clobbered a fresher secret because nobody sends check-and-set), and once when someone 'deleted' a secret and we discovered our tooling can't undelete because it doesn't know the metadata endpoints exist. I want a proper Go package, `vaultkv`, that speaks the documented KV v2 HTTP API — stdlib only, no github.com/hashicorp/vault/api, module `go-vault-kv-cas` is already set up.

Research comes first, before changing code: use WebSearch and WebFetch to read the CURRENT official Vault documentation on developer.hashicorp.com — at minimum the HTTP API overview page (base path, X-Vault-Token, X-Vault-Namespace, error document, status-code semantics) and the KV v2 secrets-engine API reference (data/delete/undelete/destroy/metadata paths, cas, version reads). Only official first-party pages count as sources. Our pinned wire contract lives in docs/contract.json with provenance in docs/official_sources.json; both are protected fixtures. Compare the live official docs against them — if anything disagrees, call that out explicitly in your summary and leave the fixtures and tests untouched. Cite the exact official URLs you actually read in your final message.

Shape of the package (package vaultkv at the module root; split it across files sensibly — I'd expect transport/errors, read/write, and version-lifecycle/metadata to live in separate files):

- New(cfg Config, hc *http.Client) *Client with Config{BaseURL, Token, Namespace, Mount string}. Every request carries the token header the docs specify; the namespace header is sent exactly when Namespace is non-empty (a root client must not send the header at all — read the API overview for what the header does). Mount is the KV v2 mount name, e.g. kv-app: all paths must be mount-aware the way the docs describe.
- Get(ctx, path) (*Secret, error) and GetVersion(ctx, path, version int) (*Secret, error) — latest read sends NO version parameter; GetVersion uses the documented query parameter. Secret{Data map[string]any; Metadata VersionMetadata} with VersionMetadata{Version int; CreatedTime, DeletionTime string; Destroyed bool}.
- Put(ctx, path, data map[string]any) (VersionMetadata, error) and PutCAS(ctx, path, data, cas int) (VersionMetadata, error) — the write body is the documented envelope; Put must omit the options object entirely, PutCAS pins options.cas (0 is meaningful — create-only — so it must be sent explicitly). Both return the write response metadata.
- DeleteLatest(ctx, path) error, DeleteVersions(ctx, path, versions []int) error, Undelete(ctx, path, versions []int) error, Destroy(ctx, path, versions []int) error — use the documented method and path for each one (they are NOT all POST, and destroy/delete/undelete do not live under data/); all succeed on 204.
- Metadata(ctx, path) (*Metadata, error) — Metadata{CurrentVersion, OldestVersion, MaxVersions int; CASRequired bool; Versions map[int]VersionMetadata}. Note the response keys the versions object by number-strings and omits the version field inside each entry; fill it from the key.
- Errors: ErrNotFound sentinel for a 404 carrying an empty errors array (per the docs that means missing OR unauthorized — preserve that ambiguity, don't invent wording). Any non-2xx with a non-empty errors array becomes *APIError{StatusCode int; Errors []string} whose Error() carries status and messages. IsCASMismatch(err) bool reports the 400 check-and-set conflict (docs/contract.json pins the message our fake returns). And the KV v2 quirk the curl scripts never handled: a 404 from the data endpoint whose body still has a data object is a soft-deleted or destroyed version — return the Secret with nil Data and the real metadata, NOT an error.

Acceptance is vaultkv_test.go — it runs a loopback fake Vault, so no real server and no real credentials are involved. Run: go test -race -timeout 30s ./... — until green. vaultkv_test.go and everything under docs/ are protected; don't touch them.

research.interface: Vault HTTP API v1 — KV secrets engine version 2 (stable mount-scoped data/delete/undelete/destroy/metadata endpoints)

official_sources.title: HTTP API | Vault | HashiCorp Developer
official_sources.used_for: /v1 base path, X-Vault-Token auth header, X-Vault-Namespace relative-path routing (header value like ns1/ns2/ prefixes the request path), the {"errors":[...]} error document, and status-code meanings — 200 success with data, 204 success with no data, 400 invalid request, 403 forbidden, 404 invalid path or no permission to view (intentionally ambiguous)

official_sources.title: KV - Secrets Engines - HTTP API | Vault | HashiCorp Developer
official_sources.used_for: mount-scoped /:mount/data|delete|undelete|destroy|metadata/:path routes, the ?version read parameter, the options.cas write field (0 = create-only, N = version must match), write/read response shapes (data.data plus data.metadata with version/created_time/deletion_time/destroyed), POST bodies {"versions":[...]} for delete/undelete and PUT for destroy, and metadata response fields (cas_required, current_version, oldest_version, max_versions, versions map keyed by version-number strings)

research.language_rationale: HashiCorp ships an official Go client (github.com/hashicorp/vault/api), but this harness is stdlib-only; the KV v2 HTTP endpoints are the documented stable integration surface — the official client is a thin wrapper over exactly these routes — and Go's net/http + encoding/json is the idiomatic zero-dependency way to build an operations tool against them.
